title: Asthma — Chronic Management
oneLiner: Chronic asthma is reversible airway obstruction driven by inflammation and hyperresponsiveness; stepwise management aims for symptom control, prevention of exacerbations, and minimal therapy side effects.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette**  
A 28-year-old non-smoker presents to GP with a 6-month history of intermittent nocturnal cough and wheeze. Symptoms are worse after exercise, in cold air, and when visiting a friend with a cat. She has a history of eczema and hay fever. On examination, she has bilateral expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiration, and a peak flow that drops by 25% when measured at 4 AM compared to her evening best. FeNO is 50 ppb. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.65 with 15% and 250 mL improvement post salbutamol.

**Dissecting the clues**  
- **Nocturnal cough + wheeze:** Diurnal variation in lung function – hallmark of asthma; worse at night due to circadian drop in cortisol and increased vagal tone.  
- **Eczema + hay fever:** Atopic background; strong risk factor for allergic asthma.  
- **Cat exposure:** Allergen trigger (Fel d 1).  
- **Exercise + cold air making worse:** Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction due to airway cooling/drying.  
- **Bilateral expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiration:** Small airways narrowed by inflammation and bronchospasm → turbulent airflow.  
- **PEF diurnal variability >20%:** Gold standard for diagnosis of asthma in the right clinical context.  
- **FeNO 50 ppb:** Elevated (normal <25 ppb) – eosinophilic airway inflammation.  
- **Post-bronchodilator reversibility >12% and >200 mL:** Demonstrates reversible component.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**  
- **Cough-variant asthma:** Only cough, no wheeze or dyspnoea – often misdiagnosed as post-nasal drip or GERD.  
- **Exercise-induced only:** Symptoms resolve fully within 30 minutes; spirometry normal at rest – need exercise challenge.  
- **Elderly-onset asthma:** Fixed airflow obstruction from long-term inflammation ± smoking – mimics COPD; confusion arises because reversibility may be incomplete.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition**  
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways that makes them twitchy and swollen. When triggered, the muscles around the airways tighten, the lining becomes more inflamed and full of mucus, and breathing becomes difficult.

**Why it matters**  
- **Frequency:** Affects ~5-10% of adults, 10-15% of children; one of the most common chronic conditions in primary care.  
- **Stakes:** 1,400 UK deaths/year from asthma – many preventable. Exacerbations cause ~100,000 hospital admissions annually.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Over-reliance on short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) without inhaled corticosteroids (ICS); poor inhaler technique; failure to recognise loss of control (e.g., nocturnal waking, increased SABA use).  
- **Economic burden:** Missed work/school days, emergency visits, biologic therapy costs.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story**  
In a susceptible person, environmental triggers (e.g., allergens, viruses) activate airway epithelial cells and dendritic cells. This leads to a Th2-predominant adaptive immune response with release of IL-4, IL-5, IL-13. These cytokines drive:  
- IgE production (mast cell sensitisation)  
- Eosinophil recruitment and activation  
- Goblet cell hyperplasia (excess mucus)  
- Airway smooth muscle hypertrophy and hyperplasia  

Acute on chronic: **early phase** (minutes) – mast cell degranulation releases histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → bronchospasm, oedema. **Late phase** (hours) – eosinophils and Th2 cells arrive → ongoing inflammation, mucus plugging, epithelial damage.

**Why you see what you see**  
- **Wheeze:** Narrowed airways → turbulent airflow during expiration (higher resistance).  
- **Prolonged expiration:** Bronchoconstriction and mucus slow airflow; the patient must exhale for longer than normal.  
- **Nocturnal symptoms:** Circadian dip in cortisol (↓ anti-inflammatory), peak vagal tone (↑ bronchoconstriction), late-phase reaction peaking at 4-6 hours after trigger.  
- **Diurnal PEF variation:** Ventilation-perfusion mismatch worsens when supine; lung function normalises during day with activity and cortisol rise.  
- **Reversibility with β-agonists:** β-2 receptors on smooth muscle → relaxation → opposite of the bronchoconstriction caused by mast cell mediators.

**Connecting to pattern recognition**  
The atopic patient’s baseline IgE is high → mast cells already loaded with allergen-specific IgE → even low-level trigger produces explosive degranulation. This explains why cat hair or grass pollen triggers immediate wheeze.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Allergens** (most common) | House dust mite, grass/tree pollen, animal dander (cat, dog) | “Worse in spring/summer” or “sleeping on old pillows” |
| **Infections** | Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), rhinovirus, influenza | “Started with a cold” – viral-induced exacerbations are the most frequent cause |
| **Irritants** | Tobacco smoke (active or passive), air pollution, strong chemical fumes | “Works in a bakery” (flour dust) or “lives near motorway” |
| **Exercise** | Cold, dry air during exertion | “Fine at rest, coughs 10 minutes into a run” |
| **Drugs** | β-blockers (even eye drops), NSAIDs (in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease [AERD]) | “Started atenolol for migraine” or “develops nasal polyps + asthma after aspirin” |
| **Occupational** | Isocyanates (painters), wood dust, flour, laboratory animals | “Symptoms improve on weekends/holidays” |
| **Emotional / stress** | Hyperventilation, altered breathing pattern | “Panic attacks trigger chest tightness” |
| **Hormonal** | Menstrual cycle (perimenstrual asthma) | “Flare-ups always just before period” |

**Highlight most common cause:** Viral upper respiratory tract infections trigger >80% of exacerbations in children and >50% in adults.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (BTS/SIGN 2019 or GINA)**  
Diagnosis requires:  
- History of variable respiratory symptoms, **plus**  
- Evidence of variable airflow obstruction on objective testing (reversibility, diurnal variability, or bronchial hyperresponsiveness)

**Key tests and interpretation**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Spirometry with reversibility** | FEV1, FVC, FEV1/FVC | Confirm obstruction + reversibility | FEV1/FVC <0.7 (obstruction); FEV1 improves >12% and >200 mL after 400 mcg salbutamol → confirms asthma |
| **Peak expiratory flow (PEF) diary** | Daily variation (morning vs evening) for 2-4 weeks | Document diurnal variability | Mean diurnal variability >20% in ≥3 days = highly supportive of asthma |
| **Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO)** | Airway eosinophilic inflammation | Identify type 2 inflammation (guides ICS/anti-IL5 use) | >50 ppb (adults) = high probability; <25 ppb = low; moderate (25-50 ppb) needs clinical correlation |
| **Blood eosinophils** | Systemic eosinophilic inflammation | Predicts response to ICS; eligibility for biologics | >300 cells/µL suggests eosinophilic phenotype; >150 for severe asthma biologics |
| **Allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE)** | Sensitisation to specific allergens | Identify triggers for avoidance; consider immunotherapy | Positive to dust mite, pollen, mould, etc. | 
| **Bronchial challenge test (methacholine/histamine)** | Airway hyperresponsiveness | Rule in asthma when spirometry normal | PC20 <8 mg/mL methacholine = positive; high negative predictive value |

**Pattern recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**  
- **Confirms:** Post-bronchodilator FEV1 improvement >12% and >200 mL in the right clinical setting (strong positive predictive value if also symptoms and no other cause).  
- **Rules out:** Normal spirometry, normal FeNO, normal PEF diary, and negative bronchial challenge test effectively exclude asthma (very high negative predictive value when combined).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour of acute exacerbation – emergency department)**  
- **Oxygen:** target SpO₂ 94-98% – hypoxaemia from V/Q mismatch; high FiO₂ needed because airways are narrowed.  
- **Short-acting β-agonist (SABA):** Salbutamol 2.5 mg via nebuliser (driven by oxygen) OR 4-6 puffs via pressurised metered-dose inhaler + spacer every 20 minutes for first hour. *Why:* Rapid bronchodilation.  
- **Oral corticosteroids:** Prednisolone 40-50 mg once daily for 5 days (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg IV stat if severe). *Why:* Suppress late-phase inflammation; takes 4-6 hours to work.  
- **Ipratropium bromide (anticholinergic):** For moderate-severe exacerbations: add 0.5 mg via nebuliser every 4-6 hours for first 24 hours. *Why:* Additional bronchodilation via vagal suppression.  
- **Magnesium sulfate (IV):** Consider single dose 1.2-2 g IV over 20 minutes if life-threatening or initial treatment fails (bronchodilator + anti-arrhythmic smooth muscle relaxation).  

**Ward (24-72 hours)**  
- Continue SABA as needed (e.g., salbutamol 2.5 mg nebulised 4-6 hourly).  
- Switch to regular **inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)** as soon as patient can use an inhaler: e.g., beclometasone dipropionate 400 mcg twice daily (or equivalent). *Why:* Relapse prevention; treat the underlying inflammation.  
- **Step-down oxygen** when SpO₂ >92% on room air.  
- **Review inhaler technique** – most patients use incorrectly. Use a spacer if using an MDI.  
- **Peak flow monitoring** three times daily: chart values; discharge when >75% of personal best.  
- **Assess triggers** – did a viral URTI start this? Consider flu vaccination.  
- **Education:** create an **asthma action plan** (green/yellow/red zone).  

**Long-term (chronic management)** – **Stepwise approach (BTS/SIGN 2019)**

| Step | Therapy | Why | Dose examples (adults) |
|------|---------|-----|-----------------------|
| 1 | SABA as needed | No daily anti-inflammatory needed; low-risk infrequent symptoms | Salbutamol 100-200 mcg via MDI as required |
| 2 | First-line: Regular low-dose ICS + SABA as needed | ICS reduces eosinophilic inflammation; prevents exacerbations | Beclometasone dipropionate 100-200 mcg twice daily (Clenil Modulite) or equivalent |
| 3 | Add LABA to low/medium dose ICS (combination inhaler) | LABA provides additional bronchodilation; synergy with ICS for better control | Fluticasone propionate 100 mcg + salmeterol 50 mcg one puff twice daily (Seretide 100) |
| 4 | Increase to medium/high-dose ICS-LABA | Uncontrolled despite step 3 | Fluticasone propionate 500 mcg + salmeterol 50 mcg one puff twice daily (Seretide 500) |
| 5 | High-dose ICS-LABA + add-on therapy (tiotropium, biologic, OCS) | Severe asthma – need phenotype-targeted therapy | Tiotropium Respimat 2.5 mcg two puffs once daily; anti-IL5 (mepolizumab 100 mg SC every 4 weeks); consider low-dose OCS (prednisolone 5-10 mg OD) |

**What NOT to do**  
- **Not:** Use LABA without ICS (increases risk of asthma death).  
- **Not:** Prescribe high-dose ICS without checking adherence and inhaler technique first.  
- **Not:** Rely on SABA monotherapy for patients using >3 canisters/year (risk of exacerbation).  
- **Not:** Ignore symptoms 3x/week or nocturnal waking – that’s uncontrolled asthma.

**Common pitfalls**  
- Non-adherence (especially ICS) – ask “how many puffs did you miss this week?”  
- Poor inhaler technique – always observe.  
- Delay stepping up therapy when symptoms persist.  
- Overuse of oral corticosteroids – consider biologics early.  

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Asthma | COPD | Vocal Cord Dysfunction (VCD) | Heart Failure (pulmonary oedema) |
|---------|--------|------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Age at onset** | Usually childhood/young adult | >40 years (smokers) | Variable, often teenage/young adult | Older adults (>65) |
| **Smoking history** | Uncommon (can be) | Strong (>20 pack-years) | No | Variable (often hypertension/IHD) |
| **Symptoms** | Intermittent wheeze, cough, chest tightness, nocturnal wakening | Progressive dyspnoea, chronic cough, sputum | Inspiratory stridor, throat tightness, ‘wheeze’ over trachea; no nocturnal symptoms | Orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, pink frothy sputum |
| **Triggers** | Allergens, exercise, cold, viruses | Exertion, infection | Stress, anxiety, strong smells; laugh/cough | Fluid overload (e.g., missed diuretic) |
| **Spirometry** | Obstructive (FEV1/FVC <0.7) with reversibility (>12% & >200 mL) | Obstructive (fixed, <0.7) with little/no reversibility | Normal; flow-volume loop shows flattened inspiratory loop | Restrictive pattern (FVC↓, FEV1/FVC normal or ↑) |
| **FeNO** | Elevated (>50 ppb) | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Imaging** | Normal (hypertension on CXR may be seen) | Hyperinflation, bullae | Normal | Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, pleural effusions |
| **Response to β-agonist** | Dramatic | Minimal | No improvement | No improvement (may worsen) |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Diurnal variability + atopy + reversibility | Fixed obstruction + smoking history | ‘Wheeze’ heard best over neck; normal PEF during attack | Crackles + raised JVP + peripheral oedema |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Acute severe asthma** | Rapidly worsening dyspnoea, inability to complete sentences, PEF <50% of best | RR >25, HR >110, SpO₂ <92%, PEF 33-50% | Immediate: O₂, neb salbutamol + ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone 200 mg, call senior |
| **Life-threatening asthma** | Silent chest (no wheeze – little air movement, severe bronchospasm), cyanosis, exhaustion, bradycardia, hypotension, SpO₂ <90%, PEF <33% | “Can’t talk” – look for accessory muscles, intercostal recession, paradoxical pulse | Escalate: call ICU/anaesthetics immediately; IV magnesium, consider non-invasive ventilation or intubation |
| **Pneumothorax** | Sudden sharp chest pain + worsening dyspnoea in a known asthmatic | Absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation if tension | Chest X-ray; if tension – needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) |
| **Atelectasis** | Persistent cough with sputum, low-grade fever, dullness on percussion | CXR shows plate-like opacities, volume loss | Chest physiotherapy, ensure adequate bronchodilation, treat underlying infection |
| **Chronic airway remodelling** | Fixed airflow obstruction (FEV1 does not fully reverse) – mimics COPD | Irreversible component; reduced exercise tolerance | Ensure optimal long-term ICS; treat as COPD if truly fixed |
| **Oral steroid side effects** | Weight gain, osteoporosis, diabetes, adrenal suppression, Cushing’s | Moon face, easy bruising, hyperglycaemia | Step down rapidly; use biologics to reduce OCS; refer to DEXA if on long-term |
| **Death** | Sudden collapse, severe hypoxia, cardiac arrest (during exacerbation) | Agonal breathing, no pulse | Full resuscitation, be aware of “near-fatal” asthma history |

**Late-presentation patterns (exam stems for untreated asthma)**  
- **“Non-adherent with ICS for 2 years, now breathless after walking 20 yards. No wheeze on auscultation. SpO₂ 85%.”** – Life-threatening asthma with silent chest → need emergency intubation.  
- **“Young asthmatic presents with pleuritic chest pain and sudden dyspnoea after coughing fit.”** – Pneumothorax.  
- **“Chronic asthmatic on high-dose ICS + LABA still waking every night – PEF diary shows 50% drop each morning.”** – Severe eosinophilic asthma; candidate for biologic therapy (mepolizumab/benralizumab/reslizumab).

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

1. **“ASTHMA”** – Stepwise escalation  
   - **A**void triggers  
   - **S**ABA as needed (step 1)  
   - **T**ake ICS regularly (step 2)  
   - **H**igher dose ICS + LABA (step 3/4)  
   - **M**onitor response  
   - **A**dd-on (tiotropium, biologic, OCS)  

2. **“SEVERE”** – Signs of severe exacerbation  
   - **S**pO₂ <92%  
   - **E**xhaustion  
   - **V**al says <33% predicted PEF  
   - **E**xpiration prolonged  
   - **R**R >25  
   - **E**xtended expiration (audible)  

3. **“SICK ASTHMA”** – Differential for acute presentation  
   - **S**erious infection (pneumonia)  
   - **I**nhaled trigger  
   - **C**ongestive heart failure  
   - **K**nown asthma – exacerbation  
   - **A**nemothorax / pneumothorax  
   - **S**moking / COPD  
   - **T**hrombotic (PE)  
   - **H**yperventilation / panic  
   - **M**edication (beta-blocker, NSAID)  
   - **A**spiration  

**Clinical pearls**  
1. **“When to escalate treatment? Use the Rule of 2s”** – Waking at night >2x/month, using SABA >2x/week, or symptoms >2x/week = poor control → step-up.  
2. **Inhaler technique matters more than drug choice.** Most patients miss 2 out of 5 steps of the classic MDI technique – teach ‘slow and deep’ not ‘fast and shallow’.  
3. **FeNO >50 ppb = eosinophilic inflammation** – strongly predicts response to ICS. Low FeNO (<25 ppb) suggests non-type-2 asthma (e.g., obese, smoking, neutrophilic) where ICS may be less effective; consider alternative diagnoses.  
4. **Occupational asthma is a medico-legal diagnosis.** Ask: “Are your symptoms better on holidays or weekends?” Refer to occupational medicine if suspected.  
5. **Biologics (anti-IL5) are not rescue therapy.** Indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma despite step 4-5 treatment; they reduce exacerbations by ~50% but do not instantly relieve symptoms.  
6. **Never use LABA alone** – covered under ‘What NOT to do’.  
7. **Peak flow diary is the cheapest and most objective way to assess control** – ask patient to bring it to every visit.

**Common mistakes to avoid**  
- Missed diagnosis: thin, young person with ‘COPD’ – recheck spirometry for reversibility.  
- Misinterpreting normal spirometry as ‘no asthma’ – need FeNO/diurnal PEF/challenge.  
- Prescribing oral prednisolone for 7 days when 5 days is sufficient for acute exacerbation (longer courses cause more side effects without added benefit).  
- Not arranging asthma review after an ED attendance – high risk of repeat if no action plan.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team**

| Situation | Specific threshold | Action |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Life-threatening features** (any one) | PEF <33% best/predicted, SpO₂ <92%, silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, hypotension, exhaustion, confusion, or coma | **Call anaesthetics/ICU NOW** – prepare for intubation; give IV magnesium 2 g over 20 min; arrange urgent transfer to critical care |
| **Severe exacerbation not improving after initial treatment** | PEF 33-50% and not improving to >75% after 1 hour of nebs + steroids | **Call senior** (med reg / respiratory consultant) – consider IV salbutamol (5 mg over 10 min then infusion 3-20 mcg/min) or IV aminophylline (only if no previous toxicity;

## Asthma — Chronic Management  
**One-liner:** Chronic asthma is reversible airway obstruction driven by inflammation and hyperresponsiveness; stepwise management aims for symptom control, prevention of exacerbations, and minimal therapy side effects.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette**  
A 28-year-old non-smoker presents to GP with a 6-month history of intermittent nocturnal cough and wheeze. Symptoms are worse after exercise, in cold air, and when visiting a friend with a cat. She has a history of eczema and hay fever. On examination, she has bilateral expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiration, and a peak flow that drops by 25% when measured at 4 AM compared to her evening best. FeNO is 50 ppb. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.65 with 15% and 250 mL improvement post salbutamol.

**Dissecting the clues**  
- **Nocturnal cough + wheeze:** Diurnal variation in lung function – hallmark of asthma; worse at night due to circadian drop in cortisol and increased vagal tone.  
- **Eczema + hay fever:** Atopic background; strong risk factor for allergic asthma.  
- **Cat exposure:** Allergen trigger (Fel d 1).  
- **Exercise + cold air making worse:** Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction due to airway cooling/drying.  
- **Bilateral expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiration:** Small airways narrowed by inflammation and bronchospasm → turbulent airflow.  
- **PEF diurnal variability >20%:** Gold standard for diagnosis of asthma in the right clinical context.  
- **FeNO 50 ppb:** Elevated (normal <25 ppb) – eosinophilic airway inflammation.  
- **Post-bronchodilator reversibility >12% and >200 mL:** Demonstrates reversible component.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**  
- **Cough-variant asthma:** Only cough, no wheeze or dyspnoea – often misdiagnosed as post-nasal drip or GERD.  
- **Exercise-induced only:** Symptoms resolve fully within 30 minutes; spirometry normal at rest – need exercise challenge.  
- **Elderly-onset asthma:** Fixed airflow obstruction from long-term inflammation ± smoking – mimics COPD; confusion arises because reversibility may be incomplete.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition**  
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways that makes them twitchy and swollen. When triggered, the muscles around the airways tighten, the lining becomes more inflamed and full of mucus, and breathing becomes difficult.

**Why it matters**  
- **Frequency:** Affects ~5-10% of adults, 10-15% of children; one of the most common chronic conditions in primary care.  
- **Stakes:** 1,400 UK deaths/year from asthma – many preventable. Exacerbations cause ~100,000 hospital admissions annually.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Over-reliance on short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) without inhaled corticosteroids (ICS); poor inhaler technique; failure to recognise loss of control (e.g., nocturnal waking, increased SABA use).  
- **Economic burden:** Missed work/school days, emergency visits, biologic therapy costs.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story**  
In a susceptible person, environmental triggers (e.g., allergens, viruses) activate airway epithelial cells and dendritic cells. This leads to a Th2-predominant adaptive immune response with release of IL-4, IL-5, IL-13. These cytokines drive:  
- IgE production (mast cell sensitisation)  
- Eosinophil recruitment and activation  
- Goblet cell hyperplasia (excess mucus)  
- Airway smooth muscle hypertrophy and hyperplasia  

Acute on chronic: **early phase** (minutes) – mast cell degranulation releases histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → bronchospasm, oedema. **Late phase** (hours) – eosinophils and Th2 cells arrive → ongoing inflammation, mucus plugging, epithelial damage.

**Why you see what you see**  
- **Wheeze:** Narrowed airways → turbulent airflow during expiration (higher resistance).  
- **Prolonged expiration:** Bronchoconstriction and mucus slow airflow; the patient must exhale for longer than normal.  
- **Nocturnal symptoms:** Circadian dip in cortisol (↓ anti-inflammatory), peak vagal tone (↑ bronchoconstriction), late-phase reaction peaking at 4-6 hours after trigger.  
- **Diurnal PEF variation:** Ventilation-perfusion mismatch worsens when supine; lung function normalises during day with activity and cortisol rise.  
- **Reversibility with β-agonists:** β-2 receptors on smooth muscle → relaxation → opposite of the bronchoconstriction caused by mast cell mediators.

**Connecting to pattern recognition**  
The atopic patient’s baseline IgE is high → mast cells already loaded with allergen-specific IgE → even low-level trigger produces explosive degranulation. This explains why cat hair or grass pollen triggers immediate wheeze.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Allergens** (most common) | House dust mite, grass/tree pollen, animal dander (cat, dog) | “Worse in spring/summer” or “sleeping on old pillows” |
| **Infections** | Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), rhinovirus, influenza | “Started with a cold” – viral-induced exacerbations are the most frequent cause |
| **Irritants** | Tobacco smoke (active or passive), air pollution, strong chemical fumes | “Works in a bakery” (flour dust) or “lives near motorway” |
| **Exercise** | Cold, dry air during exertion | “Fine at rest, coughs 10 minutes into a run” |
| **Drugs** | β-blockers (even eye drops), NSAIDs (in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease [AERD]) | “Started atenolol for migraine” or “develops nasal polyps + asthma after aspirin” |
| **Occupational** | Isocyanates (painters), wood dust, flour, laboratory animals | “Symptoms improve on weekends/holidays” |
| **Emotional / stress** | Hyperventilation, altered breathing pattern | “Panic attacks trigger chest tightness” |
| **Hormonal** | Menstrual cycle (perimenstrual asthma) | “Flare-ups always just before period” |

**Highlight most common cause:** Viral upper respiratory tract infections trigger >80% of exacerbations in children and >50% in adults.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (BTS/SIGN 2019 or GINA)**  
Diagnosis requires:  
- History of variable respiratory symptoms, **plus**  
- Evidence of variable airflow obstruction on objective testing (reversibility, diurnal variability, or bronchial hyperresponsiveness)

**Key tests and interpretation**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Spirometry with reversibility** | FEV1, FVC, FEV1/FVC | Confirm obstruction + reversibility | FEV1/FVC <0.7 (obstruction); FEV1 improves >12% and >200 mL after 400 mcg salbutamol → confirms asthma |
| **Peak expiratory flow (PEF) diary** | Daily variation (morning vs evening) for 2-4 weeks | Document diurnal variability | Mean diurnal variability >20% in ≥3 days = highly supportive of asthma |
| **Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO)** | Airway eosinophilic inflammation | Identify type 2 inflammation (guides ICS/anti-IL5 use) | >50 ppb (adults) = high probability; <25 ppb = low; moderate (25-50 ppb) needs clinical correlation |
| **Blood eosinophils** | Systemic eosinophilic inflammation | Predicts response to ICS; eligibility for biologics | >300 cells/µL suggests eosinophilic phenotype; >150 for severe asthma biologics |
| **Allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE)** | Sensitisation to specific allergens | Identify triggers for avoidance; consider immunotherapy | Positive to dust mite, pollen, mould, etc. | 
| **Bronchial challenge test (methacholine/histamine)** | Airway hyperresponsiveness | Rule in asthma when spirometry normal | PC20 <8 mg/mL methacholine = positive; high negative predictive value |

**Pattern recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**  
- **Confirms:** Post-bronchodilator FEV1 improvement >12% and >200 mL in the right clinical setting (strong positive predictive value if also symptoms and no other cause).  
- **Rules out:** Normal spirometry, normal FeNO, normal PEF diary, and negative bronchial challenge test effectively exclude asthma (very high negative predictive value when combined).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour of acute exacerbation – emergency department)**  
- **Oxygen:** target SpO₂ 94-98% – hypoxaemia from V/Q mismatch; high FiO₂ needed because airways are narrowed.  
- **Short-acting β-agonist (SABA):** Salbutamol 2.5 mg via nebuliser (driven by oxygen) OR 4-6 puffs via pressurised metered-dose inhaler + spacer every 20 minutes for first hour. *Why:* Rapid bronchodilation.  
- **Oral corticosteroids:** Prednisolone 40-50 mg once daily for 5 days (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg IV stat if severe). *Why:* Suppress late-phase inflammation; takes 4-6 hours to work.  
- **Ipratropium bromide (anticholinergic):** For moderate-severe exacerbations: add 0.5 mg via nebuliser every 4-6 hours for first 24 hours. *Why:* Additional bronchodilation via vagal suppression.  
- **Magnesium sulfate (IV):** Consider single dose 1.2-2 g IV over 20 minutes if life-threatening or initial treatment fails (bronchodilator + anti-arrhythmic smooth muscle relaxation).  

**Ward (24-72 hours)**  
- Continue SABA as needed (e.g., salbutamol 2.5 mg nebulised 4-6 hourly).  
- Switch to regular **inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)** as soon as patient can use an inhaler: e.g., beclometasone dipropionate 400 mcg twice daily (or equivalent). *Why:* Relapse prevention; treat the underlying inflammation.  
- **Step-down oxygen** when SpO₂ >92% on room air.  
- **Review inhaler technique** – most patients use incorrectly. Use a spacer if using an MDI.  
- **Peak flow monitoring** three times daily: chart values; discharge when >75% of personal best.  
- **Assess triggers** – did a viral URTI start this? Consider flu vaccination.  
- **Education:** create an **asthma action plan** (green/yellow/red zone).  

**Long-term (chronic management)** – **Stepwise approach (BTS/SIGN 2019)**

| Step | Therapy | Why | Dose examples (adults) |
|------|---------|-----|-----------------------|
| 1 | SABA as needed | No daily anti-inflammatory needed; low-risk infrequent symptoms | Salbutamol 100-200 mcg via MDI as required |
| 2 | First-line: Regular low-dose ICS + SABA as needed | ICS reduces eosinophilic inflammation; prevents exacerbations | Beclometasone dipropionate 100-200 mcg twice daily (Clenil Modulite) or equivalent |
| 3 | Add LABA to low/medium dose ICS (combination inhaler) | LABA provides additional bronchodilation; synergy with ICS for better control | Fluticasone propionate 100 mcg + salmeterol 50 mcg one puff twice daily (Seretide 100) |
| 4 | Increase to medium/high-dose ICS-LABA | Uncontrolled despite step 3 | Fluticasone propionate 500 mcg + salmeterol 50 mcg one puff twice daily (Seretide 500) |
| 5 | High-dose ICS-LABA + add-on therapy (tiotropium, biologic, OCS) | Severe asthma – need phenotype-targeted therapy | Tiotropium Respimat 2.5 mcg two puffs once daily; anti-IL5 (mepolizumab 100 mg SC every 4 weeks); consider low-dose OCS (prednisolone 5-10 mg OD) |

**What NOT to do**  
- **Not:** Use LABA without ICS (increases risk of asthma death).  
- **Not:** Prescribe high-dose ICS without checking adherence and inhaler technique first.  
- **Not:** Rely on SABA monotherapy for patients using >3 canisters/year (risk of exacerbation).  
- **Not:** Ignore symptoms 3x/week or nocturnal waking – that’s uncontrolled asthma.

**Common pitfalls**  
- Non-adherence (especially ICS) – ask “how many puffs did you miss this week?”  
- Poor inhaler technique – always observe.  
- Delay stepping up therapy when symptoms persist.  
- Overuse of oral corticosteroids – consider biologics early.  

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Asthma | COPD | Vocal Cord Dysfunction (VCD) | Heart Failure (pulmonary oedema) |
|---------|--------|------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Age at onset** | Usually childhood/young adult | >40 years (smokers) | Variable, often teenage/young adult | Older adults (>65) |
| **Smoking history** | Uncommon (can be) | Strong (>20 pack-years) | No | Variable (often hypertension/IHD) |
| **Symptoms** | Intermittent wheeze, cough, chest tightness, nocturnal wakening | Progressive dyspnoea, chronic cough, sputum | Inspiratory stridor, throat tightness, ‘wheeze’ over trachea; no nocturnal symptoms | Orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, pink frothy sputum |
| **Triggers** | Allergens, exercise, cold, viruses | Exertion, infection | Stress, anxiety, strong smells; laugh/cough | Fluid overload (e.g., missed diuretic) |
| **Spirometry** | Obstructive (FEV1/FVC <0.7) with reversibility (>12% & >200 mL) | Obstructive (fixed, <0.7) with little/no reversibility | Normal; flow-volume loop shows flattened inspiratory loop | Restrictive pattern (FVC↓, FEV1/FVC normal or ↑) |
| **FeNO** | Elevated (>50 ppb) | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Imaging** | Normal (hypertension on CXR may be seen) | Hyperinflation, bullae | Normal | Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, pleural effusions |
| **Response to β-agonist** | Dramatic | Minimal | No improvement | No improvement (may worsen) |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Diurnal variability + atopy + reversibility | Fixed obstruction + smoking history | ‘Wheeze’ heard best over neck; normal PEF during attack | Crackles + raised JVP + peripheral oedema |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Acute severe asthma** | Rapidly worsening dyspnoea, inability to complete sentences, PEF <50% of best | RR >25, HR >110, SpO₂ <92%, PEF 33-50% | Immediate: O₂, neb salbutamol + ipratropium, IV hydrocortisone 200 mg, call senior |
| **Life-threatening asthma** | Silent chest (no wheeze – little air movement, severe bronchospasm), cyanosis, exhaustion, bradycardia, hypotension, SpO₂ <90%, PEF <33% | “Can’t talk” – look for accessory muscles, intercostal recession, paradoxical pulse | Escalate: call ICU/anaesthetics immediately; IV magnesium, consider non-invasive ventilation or intubation |
| **Pneumothorax** | Sudden sharp chest pain + worsening dyspnoea in a known asthmatic | Absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation if tension | Chest X-ray; if tension – needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL) |
| **Atelectasis** | Persistent cough with sputum, low-grade fever, dullness on percussion | CXR shows plate-like opacities, volume loss | Chest physiotherapy, ensure adequate bronchodilation, treat underlying infection |
| **Chronic airway remodelling** | Fixed airflow obstruction (FEV1 does not fully reverse) – mimics COPD | Irreversible component; reduced exercise tolerance | Ensure optimal long-term ICS; treat as COPD if truly fixed |
| **Oral steroid side effects** | Weight gain, osteoporosis, diabetes, adrenal suppression, Cushing’s | Moon face, easy bruising, hyperglycaemia | Step down rapidly; use biologics to reduce OCS; refer to DEXA if on long-term |
| **Death** | Sudden collapse, severe hypoxia, cardiac arrest (during exacerbation) | Agonal breathing, no pulse | Full resuscitation, be aware of “near-fatal” asthma history |

**Late-presentation patterns (exam stems for untreated asthma)**  
- **“Non-adherent with ICS for 2 years, now breathless after walking 20 yards. No wheeze on auscultation. SpO₂ 85%.”** – Life-threatening asthma with silent chest → need emergency intubation.  
- **“Young asthmatic presents with pleuritic chest pain and sudden dyspnoea after coughing fit.”** – Pneumothorax.  
- **“Chronic asthmatic on high-dose ICS + LABA still waking every night – PEF diary shows 50% drop each morning.”** – Severe eosinophilic asthma; candidate for biologic therapy (mepolizumab/benralizumab/reslizumab).

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**

1. **“ASTHMA”** – Stepwise escalation  
   - **A**void triggers  
   - **S**ABA as needed (step 1)  
   - **T**ake ICS regularly (step 2)  
   - **H**igher dose ICS + LABA (step 3/4)  
   - **M**onitor response  
   - **A**dd-on (tiotropium, biologic, OCS)  

2. **“SEVERE”** – Signs of severe exacerbation  
   - **S**pO₂ <92%  
   - **E**xhaustion  
   - **V**al says <33% predicted PEF  
   - **E**xpiration prolonged  
   - **R**R >25  
   - **E**xtended expiration (audible)  

3. **“SICK ASTHMA”** – Differential for acute presentation  
   - **S**erious infection (pneumonia)  
   - **I**nhaled trigger  
   - **C**ongestive heart failure  
   - **K**nown asthma – exacerbation  
   - **A**nemothorax / pneumothorax  
   - **S**moking / COPD  
   - **T**hrombotic (PE)  
   - **H**yperventilation / panic  
   - **M**edication (beta-blocker, NSAID)  
   - **A**spiration  

**Clinical pearls**  
1. **“When to escalate treatment? Use the Rule of 2s”** – Waking at night >2x/month, using SABA >2x/week, or symptoms >2x/week = poor control → step-up.  
2. **Inhaler technique matters more than drug choice.** Most patients miss 2 out of 5 steps of the classic MDI technique – teach ‘slow and deep’ not ‘fast and shallow’.  
3. **FeNO >50 ppb = eosinophilic inflammation** – strongly predicts response to ICS. Low FeNO (<25 ppb) suggests non-type-2 asthma (e.g., obese, smoking, neutrophilic) where ICS may be less effective; consider alternative diagnoses.  
4. **Occupational asthma is a medico-legal diagnosis.** Ask: “Are your symptoms better on holidays or weekends?” Refer to occupational medicine if suspected.  
5. **Biologics (anti-IL5) are not rescue therapy.** Indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma despite step 4-5 treatment; they reduce exacerbations by ~50% but do not instantly relieve symptoms.  
6. **Never use LABA alone** – covered under ‘What NOT to do’.  
7. **Peak flow diary is the cheapest and most objective way to assess control** – ask patient to bring it to every visit.

**Common mistakes to avoid**  
- Missed diagnosis: thin, young person with ‘COPD’ – recheck spirometry for reversibility.  
- Misinterpreting normal spirometry as ‘no asthma’ – need FeNO/diurnal PEF/challenge.  
- Prescribing oral prednisolone for 7 days when 5 days is sufficient for acute exacerbation (longer courses cause more side effects without added benefit).  
- Not arranging asthma review after an ED attendance – high risk of repeat if no action plan.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team**

| Situation | Specific threshold | Action |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Life-threatening features** (any one) | PEF <33% best/predicted, SpO₂ <92%, silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, hypotension, exhaustion, confusion, or coma | **Call anaesthetics/ICU NOW** – prepare for intubation; give IV magnesium 2 g over 20 min; arrange urgent transfer to critical care |
| **Severe exacerbation not improving after initial treatment** | PEF 33-50% and not improving to >75% after 1 hour of nebs + steroids | **Call senior** (med reg / respiratory consultant) – consider IV salbutamol (5 mg over 10 min then infusion 3-20 mcg/min) or IV aminophylline (only if no previous toxicity;
